coredata.dc:description:
In the current work, a novel approach is developed to modulate the photocatalytic performance of titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) via Ni doping coupled with the decoration of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In-depth analysis using advanced techniques such as Inductively Coupled Plasma – Mass Spectroscopy (ICP-MS) and High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) reveals the composition of CQDs with TiO2 NPs and, particularly, the doping of single Nickel (Ni) atoms into the TiO2 structure, which leads to the formation of oxygen vacancies. The synergetic effect of Ni doping and CQDs decoration results in efficient visible light absorption and charge separation. Furthermore, electrochemical techniques such as Transient Photocurrent Response (TPR) and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) were utilized to assess the electron transport efficiency under simulated light, providing insights that contributed to the proposed reaction mechanism. As a result, the prepared material exhibits enhanced degradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin (CIP), reaching up to 97 % after 150 min under irradiation by low-power household LED. The degradation reaction followed first-order kinetics, with a rate constant of k = 0.02222 min−1, and reactive species such as hydroxyl radicals (
OH
∙
) and superoxide radicals (
O 2
∙ −
) played crucial roles in the reaction mechanism. This work provides a promising approach for the design of nanomaterials for efficient degradation of pollutants via photocatalysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Homemade photoreactor used for the photocatalytic degradation of CIP. The reactor is equipped with three household LED lamps (5000 K) with total power of 60 W.
Characterization of the synthesized materials: (a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, (c) FT-IR spectra, and (d) UV–Vis DRS spectra.
XPS survey (A), high-resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p (B); C 1s (C); O 1s (D), and Valance Zone (E) of the NTC sample.
The EDX spectrum, and elemental mapping of Ti, C, and O elements of NTC.
High Resolution-TEM images and corresponding interplanar space of (A) TiO2 pure; (B) Ni-doped TiO2; (C) NTC; (D) CQDs; (E) NTC (other position); (F) TiO2 pure (other position).

Photocatalytic Performance and Characterization: (a) Antibiotic Degradation Efficiency Over Time, (b) Kinetic Analysis of the Reaction, (c) Photoluminescence (PL) Spectra, and (d) Effect of Radical Trapping on Degradation Efficiency of Materials, (e) Stable photocurrent response curve toward on/off cycles of simulated sunlight illumination, (f) and EIS Nyquist plots spectra of different modified electrodes.

Proposed mechanism for the photodegradation of CIP over NTC photocatalyst: Schematic representation of the semiconductor catalyst with the involved electronic transitions upon photon absorption, as well as the corresponding redox potentials and reaction sequence for the oxidative degradation of CIP upon reduction at the excited photocatalyst.
Phase composition and average particle size of TiO2 and NTC sample.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In the current work, a novel approach is developed to modulate the photocatalytic performance of titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) via Ni doping coupled with the decoration of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In-depth analysis using advanced techniques such as Inductively Coupled Plasma – Mass Spectroscopy (ICP-MS) and High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) reveals the composition of CQDs with TiO2 NPs and, particularly, the doping of single Nickel (Ni) atoms into the TiO2 structure, which leads to the formation of oxygen vacancies. The synergetic effect of Ni doping and CQDs decoration results in efficient visible light absorption and charge separation. Furthermore, electrochemical techniques such as Transient Photocurrent Response (TPR) and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) were utilized to assess the electron transport efficiency under simulated light, providing insights that contributed to the proposed reaction mechanism. As a result, the prepared material exhibits enhanced degradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin (CIP), reaching up to 97 % after 150 min under irradiation by low-power household LED. The degradation reaction followed first-order kinetics, with a rate constant of k = 0.02222 min−1, and reactive species such as hydroxyl radicals (
OH
∙
) and superoxide radicals (
O 2
∙ −
) played crucial roles in the reaction mechanism. This work provides a promising approach for the design of nanomaterials for efficient degradation of pollutants via photocatalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, pharmaceuticals have been considered an urgent environmental issue. They are continuously being used extensively, and their ability to self-degrade is deficient. Both pharmaceuticals and their metabolites are introduced into aquatic environments through human excretion. Despite the use of sewage treatment plants, antibiotic residues remain a significant problem in water as they do not have sufficient capacity to degrade these compounds in wastewater effectively. Thus, with their difficult degradability, about 70 % of them cannot be degraded entirely or transformed [1]. Finally, this type of pharmaceutical always appears in the aquatic ecosystem, even at trace concentrations. Kinny and colleagues reported, “Even at low concentrations, these pharmaceuticals and their secondary products cause significant environmental damage to the existing ecosystems and populations” [2]. As a result, pharmaceuticals become a cycle with water sources. In this cycle, pharmaceuticals spread into other water sources (surface waters, groundwater, and sediment layers) [3]. Wastewater treatment plants have reported large quantities of pharmaceutical residues in water (including both natural and drinking water), ranging from ng/L to several mg/L, with some cases reaching up to several mg/L [4,5]. The widespread use of pharmaceuticals, including fluoroquinolones, poses a significant environmental risk due to their low degradability and potential for drug-resistant gene development. Fluoroquinolones are among the most important and widely used types of drugs worldwide. These drugs have various purposes, such as ensuring human health or serving veterinary purposes [6,7]. These broad-spectrum antibiotics in the environment are always a potential risk to bacterial species. This is because bacteria can quickly develop drug-resistant genes, the spread of which directly threatens the ecosystem and human health [8,9]. CIP is a typical fluoroquinolone antibiotic; therefore, this antibiotic is detected primarily in wastewater, surface water, groundwater, and drinking water [3]. Removing antibiotic pollutants from water sources has become a mandatory task for researchers.

Various technologies have been applied to treat and remove these pollutants, including biological methods [10], physical-chemical methods, and oxidation methods. However, most of these methods have limitations, such as secondary pollution, high maintenance costs, and complex processing procedures [11]. Some studies have indicated that treatment methods such as adsorption, nanofiltration (NF), reverse osmosis (RO), and granular activated carbon (GAC) have effectively removed pollutants from water [12,13]. Over the last decades, photocatalysis has gained widespread attention due to its potential in environmental remediation and energy conversion. This is an advanced process involving the generation of charge carriers, including electrons (e−) and holes (h+), via the light-triggered excitation of semiconducting materials. These charge carriers initiate the redox reactions of H2O and O2 to form highly reactive oxygen species, which can readily oxidize and degrade organic pollutants to non-toxic compounds or even CO2 and H2O [14–19]. Regarding the potential of photocatalysis, various photocatalysts have been developed and applied for antibiotic removal in water, such as metal oxides, sulphides, sillenites, as well as nanocomposites of these materials [14,20–23].

TiO2 NPs have been known for their advanced features, including chemical stability, good photocatalytic activity, wide availability, and particularly low toxicity. However, it demonstrates photocatalytic activity only under ultraviolet light due to its considerable bandgap energy (approximately 3.2 eV for the anatase phase and 3.0 eV for the rutile phase). Therefore, this material can only absorb a small portion of the solar spectrum because only about 3–5 % of sunlight is in the ultra-violet range. Incorporating other photocatalytic materials onto the surface of TiO2 NPs as cocatalysts is a straightforward modification approach to enhance the photocatalytic performance of these NPs. While some precious metals have been used, their high cost significantly impacts commercial and large-scale applications. Therefore, there is a need to develop low-cost cocatalyst materials. CQDs have emerged as a promising option. Specifically, CQDs possess a suitable bandgap, unique electron donor/acceptor properties, and exceptional electronic conduction characteristics, making them highly effective for improving the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2. CQDs exhibit extraordinary up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL), an optical phenomenon where the emitted light has a shorter wavelength than the excitation light. This feature is advantageous because CQDs absorb longer-wavelength light and emit shorter-wavelength light, which complements the absorption characteristics of TiO2 [24,25]. In other words, incorporating CQDs helps overcome the limitations of TiO2's photocatalytic applications, which typically suffer from poor utilization of sunlight. Structural modification by doping foreign atoms of metals, such as Ni, into the TiO2 structure is another widely used approach to improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 NPs [26–28]. The doping can create additional states within the bandgap of TiO2, leading to a narrowed bandgap and consequent improved light absorption. Notably, the presence of doped metal atoms can result in crystal defects, prominently oxygen vacancies, which improve the separation and transfer of charge carriers, further enhancing photocatalytic activity. In particular, the combination of CQD incorporation and Ni doping should bring a superior improvement in the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 NPs compared with each method alone, thanks to the synergistic effect. To date, this combination has not been studied for the photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics.

Therefore, the present study developed a new strategy involving structural modification of TiO2 NPs by coupling CQD incorporation with Ni doping to enhance photocatalytic activity for CIP degradation. Ni doping was achieved using the sol-gel method, which interestingly introduced a low concentration of single Ni atoms into the TiO2 structure. CQDs were robustly grafted onto Ni-doped TiO2 thanks to the electrostatic interaction between the functional groups on the surface of TiO2 and CQDs under thermal support. Decorating CQDs with their intrinsic UCPL effect not only improved visible light absorption but also supported the photoactivation of Ni-doped TiO2, promoting the generation of charge carriers while facilitating their separation via interfacial charge transfer. At the same time, the doping of single Ni atoms, although not reducing the TiO2 bandgap, led to the formation of oxygen vacancies, which further enhanced the charge separation efficiency. These improvements worked synergistically, resulting in efficient charge separation with a significantly reduced recombination rate that advanced rapid-kinetic photocatalytic degradation of CIP under LED light.

The chemicals used in this study include Titanium (IV) tetra isopropoxide (TTIP, 97 %), Nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, 99.99 %); sucrose (purity 99.5 %), ethanol absolute (EtOH) - (C2H5OH, >99,7 %); ciprofloxacin (CIP, 98 % - Merck) were from Sigma Aldrich (Merck) and were used without further purification. All solutions were prepared using deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by one-pot hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CQDs was described in reports before [29,30]. Briefly, 50 mL of 5.0 % sucrose solution (in a mixture of water and EtOH with a ratio of 3:2 by v/v) was transferred into a 200 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h at a heating rate of 2 °C/min. The obtained suspension was centrifugated at 12,000 rpm to remove a dark brown solid containing particles with a large size. The liquid phase was dialyzed by a dialysis membrane (Spectra/Por® Biotech CE MWCO 100–500D) before dried at 80 °C for 24 h. The obtained brown solid was re-distributed in water to get a CQDs dispersion system for further experiments.

For the preparation of the sample 0.05 % Ni (molar ratio to Ti), first, 50 mL of NiCl2 solution (5 mg·mL) and 1.78 g titanium precursor TTIP was added into 30 mL of a mixture solvent (EtOH: H2O = 1:2 by v/v). The mixture was stirred at 500 rpm for 15 min, and the product was separated from the liquid phase by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The solid was dried at 100 °C overnight (about 12 h). The obtained solid was calcinated at 400 °C for 4 h (at a heating rate of 10 °C·min). Finally, it was cooled down to room temperature naturally, and the final solid was ground using agate mortar and pestle to make a fine powder. Other ratios of Nickel in Ni-doped TiO2 materials were also prepared using the same procedure.

The synthesis procedure was described as follows (Figure Supporting Information 1 - Fig. SI. 1): 0.5 g of Ni-doped TiO2 was well dispersed in 30 mL of a mixture containing EtOH and H2O (ratio of 1:2 v/v) using ultrasonication (160 W–40 kHz) for 10 min. Subsequently, 5.6 mL of a CQDs dispersion system (the concentration of 0.95 mg·mL−1) was slowly added to the mixture and continuously stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Then, the resulting mixture was vaporized and dried at 200 °C for 12 h. The obtained powder was ground to a fine powder, denoted as NTC, standing for Ni-doped TiO2@CQDs.

The crystal structure of the as-prepared materials was examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD) that was performed on XRD (Rigaku MiniFlex™ 600) with a CuKα radiation source (1.540593 Å) at an accelerated voltage of 40 kV, a selection current of 44 mA and a step size of 0.01° and a scan rate of 5°·min−1. The surface morphology and the composition of the NTC sample were observed using Scanning Electron Microscopy – Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy mapping (SEM-EDX mapping) with a JSM microscope (JEOL, Japan). The information on the microstructures (particle size and shape, crystal quality, and morphology) of samples was characterized by HR-TEM-EDX using JEM 2100 (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS) were performed using the Cary 5000 version 3.07 instrument. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were manipulated on the HRS400 and S-CMOS Kuro devices (Princeton) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) technique. (Cary 630, Agilent Technologies) to identify the surface functional groups of the samples. Raman spectra were measured at λ = 532 nm using Thermo Scientific DXR3 Raman Spectrometers.

To gain information on the valent state of the element and chemical bonding in the obtained materials, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a Kratos Axis Ultra XPS (Shimadzu) with an Al (Kα) X-ray source whose energy was 1486.6 eV and the step of 50.0 meV. A charge neutralizer was used at 1.6 A (balance at 3.8 V with bias of 1.0 V). The obtained data was adjusted to the core level value (284.8 eV) of the C(1s). The specific surface areas and the average pore size distribution of the materials were determined by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method using a Micromeritics® ASAP 2060 (Accelerated Surface Area and Porosimetry, 2060 serial) system with nitrogen as analysis adsorptive analysis bath temperature of around 77.3 K. The samples were degassed for 2 h at around 150 °C before being weighed and analyzed. The presence of Ni element was determined using the ICP-MS technique (Thermoscientific ICP-MS instrument, model: iCAP RQ) with the standard mode for Nickel trace detection.

The photocatalytic degradation test was carried out using homemade photoreactor systems equipped with 60 W household LEDs (5000 K) purchased from the local store, as shown in Fig. 1 . To determine the photocatalytic efficiency of the material, 5.0 mg of the powder was added to 5.0 mL of 20 ppm CIP. The mixture was then illuminated under continuous stirring at 200 rpm. The reaction was carried out for 150 min. The CIP concentrations at initial (C 0) and after an interval time (C t) of the reaction were analyzed using a Cary 60 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer.

The mineralization of CIP was tested via the change in the chemical oxygen demand (COD) value of reaction solutions with a potassium dichromate standard method (as described in SI. 2.2 section). The reusability and stability of the materials were tested for 4 cycles. The procedure was as follows: after a photocatalytic reaction, the entire mixture was centrifuged at a speed of 10,000 rpm for 10 min to separate the solid catalyst and the liquid phase. After removing the liquid, this solid was washed at least 3 times with distilled water using a similar centrifugation process. Finally, the sediment was collected, dried at 100 °C for 12 h, and used for the next cycle.

Fig. 2C shows the results of the FT-IR spectra of NTC and two comparison samples, TiO2 and Ni-doped TiO2. The broadest bands in the range of 3000–3500 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of the OH bonds of all materials, including the OH bonds on the surface of TiO2 or the OH groups in the structure of CQDs [37,38]. The signal intensity of these groups increases in the order of TiO2, Ni-doped TiO2, and NTC, indicating an increase in the number of
OH −
groups on the material's surface. The peak at 1650 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending mode of water molecules. The signal intensity of the
OH −
groups in the doped samples is higher than in undoped samples. This observation suggests these defect sites can adsorb and subsequently dissociate H2O molecules by transferring a proton to a nearby oxygen atom. Therefore, each oxygen vacancy can create two hydroxyl groups for the adsorption of H2O molecules [39]. The bands at 2800 cm−1 and 1480 cm−1 are assigned to the vibrations of the CH and CO functional groups, respectively. This implies that several hydrophilic functional groups are present on the surface of the materials. Finally, the strong absorption band in the region below 1000 cm−1 shows the metal oxide bonds of Ti, including TiOTi or NiOTi bonds [40]. At 1414 cm−1 and 1795 cm−1, these signals correspond to the bending vibrations and stretching vibrations of the
COO −
group. These peaks are attributed to the residual functional groups from the TTIP precursor or surface groups on TiO2 [41], and the intensity of these signals increases with the incorporation of Ni. Upon the introduction of CQDs, the intensity of these signals significantly decreases, with a shift to 1440 cm−1, and the signal at 1795 cm−1 is eliminated, likely due to the interaction of CQDs with the surface groups on TiO2 [42]. Additionally, a new signal appears at 1526 cm−1, corresponding to the CC vibration. Furthermore, in the 1000–1200 cm−1 region, new signals emerge only in the presence of CQDs, which could be attributed to the CC and COC vibrations, indicating the presence of CQDs. Moreover, the significant decrease in PL intensity of the NTC sample compared to CQDs suggests electron transfer between Ni-doped TiO2 and CQDs (Fig. 6C).

The doping of Ni atoms in the TiO2 crystal structure was determined by Raman spectroscopy. As observed in Fig. 2B, the Raman spectrum of pure TiO2 material shows five bands corresponding to six Raman active modes of the material. The Raman peaks are well resolved for the D4h point group at around 145, 197, 645 cm−1 (Eg); 520 cm−1 (A1g + B1g), and 396 cm−1 (B1g). These peaks are corresponding to the observed anatase phase of TiO2. The Eg modes primarily correspond to the symmetric stretching vibrations of the OTiO bonds. Next, B1g modes are associated with the symmetric bending vibrations of the OTiO bonds. Lastly, the A1g mode is linked explicitly to the asymmetric bending vibration of the OTiO bonds within TiO2 [36]. At 520 cm−1, under normal conditions, the A1g and B1g (2) peaks manifest as a unified composite peak owing to their close frequencies. Consequently, an individual peak becomes dominant, resulting in the fact that it is merely to observe the other five oscillation peaks, as illustrated in Fig. 2B. In the Raman spectra of Ni-doped materials, the absence of Ni peaks and corresponding Ni oxide crystal peaks may be due to the low concentration of the Ni dopant. This indicates that individual Nickel compounds are not present in the doped materials, a conclusion that aligns with the XRD results. Notably, when looking at insight into the Eg (1) peak at 145 cm−1 of Raman spectra, it can be seen the reduction of the peak intensity may be due to the interaction of TiO2 and the modifiers (i.e. Ni dopant or CQDs). In the Ni-doped materials, the presence of Ni affects the TiO2 resonance signal on the Raman spectrum, leading to a decrease in the resonance signal and then a reduction in the intensity of the Raman peaks [43]. Besides, the peak shift of Eg (1) mode, the variations in its width can be observed upon the presence of foreign species that can be attributed to specific factors such as the change of particle size [44], the presence of non-stoichiometric oxygen deficiencies [45], photon confinement [46], and 11-phonon overtone effects [47]. The impact of the particle size change is insignificant; therefore, the observed Raman mode shifts and width changes may be deduced from the difference in valence states between Ni2+ and Ti4+ ions. Because Ti4+ has a higher oxidation state than Ni2+, substituting Ti4+ ions with Ni2+ ions generates oxygen vacancies to neutralize the charge imbalance. This leads to Raman mode shifts and width changes [45]. Furthermore, the formation of bonds involving the doped element (Ni) within the TiO2 structure, such as O-Ni-O and Ni-O-Ti bonds, may potentially impact the polarity of the dominant O-Ti-O bonds in TiO2, and it may contribute to the observed changes in Raman spectra [48].

Fig. 2D presents the UV–Vis DRS spectra of the three material samples (pure TiO2, Ni-doped TiO2, and NTC). The Tauc-plot (Fig. SI. 2) was calculated and analyzed from this data to determine the specific bandgap values for each material. According to the analysis in Fig. SI. 2, it appears that Ni doping does not directly result in a narrowing between the VB and CB gap but instead affects the material through the formation of oxygen vacancies due to the deficient concentration of Ni. The improved light absorption likely arises from the decoration of CQDs, which induces a red shift and enhances light absorption in the visible region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Textural and morphological properties of the NTC nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. SI. 3 shows the isotherms of N2 adsorption-desorption of the three samples. Both pure TiO2 and TiO2-CQDs show the isotherm curves with the adsorption and desorption lines are almost identical. In contrast, Ni-doped TiO2 and NTC exhibit the onset of observable hysteresis loops. Nevertheless, the extent of hysteresis is relatively small and falls within the relative pressure
P
P 0
range of 0.6÷0.8, indicating the existence of porous structures. Table 3 summarizes the textural parameters of all samples, demonstrating that all the synthesized materials have a specific surface area ranging from approximately 60÷120 m2·g−1. The presence of CQDs increased the surface area of the materials. The combination of a high specific surface area and porous structures contributes favorably to the adsorption and photocatalytic processes. The larger specific surface area provides more active catalytic sites, thus promoting the reaction kinetics [63].

The TEM images (Fig. SI. 4A) of NTC reveal the morphology of TiO2 particles, which possess a spherical-like shape (anatase phase) with a small size of about 9–14 nm. The TiO2 nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) with numerous OH groups on the surface can aggregate into clusters. The size of TiO2 NPs was determined using Image J software, as shown in Fig. SI. 4B. The estimated average size of TiO2 NPs is 10.3 nm, which is in good agreement with the XRD result of 10.4 nm. This value confirms the success of the sol-gel method in synthesizing TiO2 nanoparticles. Moreover, Fig. SI. 4B presents the NTC particle size distribution graph, indicating a relatively broad range of particle sizes (ranging from 3 to 21 nm) with a predominant concentration of particles in the 9–14 nm range. This observation highlights the essential uniformity of the NTC material sample.

To explore the crystal structure in more detail, HR-TEM analysis was performed on both pure TiO2 and Ni-doped samples, all calcined at 400 °C. The micrographs for pure TiO2 demonstrate spherical particles with the anatase crystal phase, showing a d-spacing of 3.41 Å, which corresponds to the lattice spacing of the [101] planes of the pristine anatase phase [64], shown in (Fig. 5A). Upon introducing the dopant into the TiO2 lattice, the crystallites became larger, consistent with the XRD results. As shown in Fig. 5B–C, the d-spacing of the [101] plane expands by >0.1 Å in the doped samples, implicitly confirming the presence of Ni within the TiO2 lattice. Notably, the addition of CQDs in the NTC sample does not alter the crystal lattice structure of Ni-doped TiO2. CQDs, which were challenging to observe in earlier TEM images due to their minute size, are visible in the HR-TEM analysis. These CQDs exhibit a d-spacing of 2.32 Å, attributed to the lattice spacing of the [002] planes [65], (Fig. 5D). The distribution of Ni species at the atomic scale is evident when comparing HR-TEM images of NTC and pure TiO2, as shown in Fig. 5E and F, respectively. In bright field imaging, atomic positions appear as darker regions, but the small difference in atomic numbers (Z) between Ni (Z = 28) and Ti (Z = 22) makes identifying individual atoms challenging. However, single Ni atoms, highlighted by yellow circles, can be discerned using the ‘Invert’ function, which enhances the contrast of profile cuts. These profiles reveal distinct bright spots that are larger and more prominent compared to the surrounding areas and are absent in the pure TiO2 sample. The analysis of light intensity and the size of these spots further indicate that the positions of Ni and Ti atoms are not clearly classified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic properties of the NTC material
ce:section.ce:para: The experiments for examining the photocatalytic activity of NTC compared to other samples in the degradation of CIP under the same experimental conditions are presented in Fig. 6A. Without a catalyst, there was almost no change in the concentration of CIP under visible light, as CIP is a persistent compound that is difficult to degrade under normal conditions. Consequently, the presence of CIP in wastewater poses a significant threat to the ecosystem. When comparing the efficiency of commercial TiO2 (P25), its catalytic efficiency (27 %) was found to be much lower than that of the as-synthesized TiO2 (60 %). The presence of Ni in the Ni-doped TiO2 material enhanced the CIP treatment efficiency by up to 80 % after 150 min of the visible radiation compared to the value of 60 % in the case of pure TiO2. Similarly, when CQDs were present, the photocatalytic efficiency of CIP degradation increased both in TiO2-CQDs and NTC samples. The combination of Ni-doped TiO2 and CQDs especially led to an increase in the CIP degradation yield of up to 97 % under LED visible light with a kinetic constant defined as 0.02222 min−1. At the same time, the COD removal result of NTC was also significantly improved from 17 % to 80 % compared to pure TiO2 (Table 2). The charge separation and transport processes were then analyzed using photoluminescence (PL) techniques (Fig. 6C). The PL intensity of the NTC heterojunction was suppressed compared with single-component samples, including CQDs, P25, and as-prepared TiO2, illustrating more efficient separation of electrons and holes, which might be derived from the capture ability of the abundant surface hydroxyl groups over holes [66]. The photocatalytic activity of TiO2 samples combined with CQDs was significantly increased compared to pure TiO2 samples (P25 or TiO2), explained by the UCPL effect of CQDs [24,25].

To determine the generation of reactive species and their participation in the photocatalytic degradation of CIP, capture experiments, which involve the addition of scavengers during photocatalytic testing, were carried out. The scavengers employed were as follows: 4–hydroxybenzoic acid (as the scavenger of
O
H ads
∙
[67]), formic acid (as the scavenger the hole in the valence band (
h VB +
) [68]), tert-butanol (as the scavenger of
O
H ads
∙
[69]), and hydroquinone (as the scavenger of
O 2
∙ −
ads
[70]). As observed in Fig. 6D, the addition of all scavengers led to a substantial decrease in the photodegradation efficiency, revealing the generation and participation of different radicals in the degradation of CIP, prominently
O
H ads
∙
and
O 2
∙ −
ads
. Moreover,
h VB +
directly participated in the CIP degradation. However, the difference in decreasing degree caused by these scavengers is slight, suggesting that they all played a significant role in the CIP degradation. Upon the scavenger addition, reactions between photogenerated radicals and these scavengers were favored, leading to a significant depletion of radicals and, thus decreased efficiency of the CIP photodegradation. Besides removing radicals generated during photocatalysis, the process of transforming potent free radicals into weaker ones could be one of the reasons for this decreased efficiency.

The recovery and reusability of the catalyst were tested by performing the photocatalytic process in 4 cycles. The efficiency of the CIP degradation in 4 cycles is illustrated in Fig. SI. 5. The results showed that the catalyst activity shows an insignificant decrease, <10 %, after 4 cycles with a relatively sizeable experimental sample size. This indicates that the catalyst is almost completely regenerated after each cycle. At the same time, the binding of CQDs to Ni-doped TiO2 is strong enough to prevent CQDs [55] from being separated from the catalyst system after at least 4 cycles.

For the analysis of charge separation and charge carrier dynamics in TiO2, Ni-doped TiO2, and NTC samples, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent measurements under simulated light on/off conditions were conducted. The impedance of the materials was determined based on the curvature radius of the EIS Nyquist plots. According to theory, “the smaller the radius, the lower the impedance, and the higher the efficiency of photogenerated carrier separation.” [71]. As shown in Fig. 6F, the smaller arc radius of the NTC material compared to TiO2 and Ni-doped TiO2 on the Nyquist plot indicates a higher separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, attributed to the synergistic effect between CQDs and Ni-doped TiO2. The electron-hole separation capability of these materials was further evaluated using photocurrent measurements under simulated light conditions (as shown in Fig. 6E). The photocurrent intensity of NTC is significantly higher than that of Ni-doped TiO2 and exhibits distinct shapes compared to pure semiconductors like TiO2. This result further corroborates the evaluations from EIS and PL, demonstrating that the presence of Ni within the TiO2 structure combined with CQDs facilitates multiple electron pathways, reducing electron-hole recombination and extending the charge carrier's lifetime. This directly impacts the photocatalytic efficiency of the material. However, the PL intensity of NTC is higher than that of Ni-doped TiO2, likely due to the occurrence of non-radiative recombination processes [72], and trap-mediated recombination [73], which influence PL intensity despite the relatively small signal difference.

To investigate the photocatalytic degradation process of CIP, the experiment was conducted under normal conditions, illuminated for 150 min, without the addition of any specific conditions. As known, photocatalytic activity is the outcome of the formation of electron-hole pairs (
e CB −
and
h VB +
) within semiconducting materials upon photon absorption. According to the Tauc-plot, Eg (NTC) = 2.98 eV, XPS valance zone, the VB potential of NTC is analyzed as (+1.79 eV), then the CB potential of NTC is calculated as (−1.19 eV). The CB and VB potentials of TiO2 are calculated according to the following equation [74]:
(1)
E VB
=
E CB
+
E g
where E
g , E
VB , and E
CB are the bandgap energy and the electron energy of the valance and conductor band of the semiconductor, respectively.

The proposed reaction mechanism can be outlined as follows (as shown in Fig. 7 ): Under LED light in the 420–780 nm range, Ni-doped TiO2 is almost inactive due to its large bandgap. In this case, CQDs play a crucial role due to their intrinsic UCPL effect [24,25]. The CQDs absorb long-wavelength light and emit shorter-wavelength light, which is suitable for activating NTC. Upon absorbing visible light, the photogenerated electrons (
e CB −
) and holes
h VB +
within the CQDs separate, forming conduction bands (CB) and valence bands (VB). Here, electrons can migrate to the CB of Ni-doped TiO2 while holes remain in the VB of the CQDs. The holes can then react with H2O to generate hydroxyl radicals (
OH
∙
ads
). Simultaneously, the electrons migrating to the CB of Ni-doped TiO2 combine with dissolved oxygen (O2) to form superoxide radicals (
O 2
∙ −
ads
). The combination of (
OH
∙
ads
) and (
O 2
∙ −
ads
) greatly enhances the efficiency of CIP degradation. The formation of these reactive oxygen species (ROS) was confirmed by radical scavenger experiments (Fig. 6D). In addition to the primary radicals that play a dominant role in the CIP degradation process,
h VB +
also contributes significantly to the efficiency of CIP degradation through specific reactions [72]. Additionally, the interfacial bonding between Ni-doped TiO2 and CQDs plays a pivotal role in promoting the separation of
e CB −
and
h VB +
[72,75], as corroborated by the results from PL, EIS, and TPR. Furthermore, the doping of Ni into the TiO2 structure creates oxygen vacancy sites, which act as electron traps, enhancing charge transfer and separation efficiency. The XPS results and the improved CIP degradation performance further validate this proposed analysis.

Once formed on the catalyst surface, these highly reactive oxygen species (ROS) continue to attack intermediate products, leading to ring-opening and the production of
F −
,
NO 3 −
,
HCOO −
, and
CH 3
COO −
ions. Ultimately, these inorganic and organic acid ions can be mineralized into CO2 and H2O [76], as presented in the reaction mechanism.

This work has unveiled the significant potential of Ni-doped TiO2 integrated with CQDs at an ultra-low Ni concentration (0.05%wt) for the photodegradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) under sunlight. A critical breakthrough of this study is the discovery of Ni existing in a single-atom state within the TiO2 structure, as confirmed by advanced techniques like ICP-MS and HR-TEM. This single-atom configuration of Ni is pivotal, contributing to the remarkable photocatalytic efficiency of the material, which achieved 97 % degradation of CIP within 150 min, following first-order kinetics. This work further elucidates the crucial roles of hydroxyl radicals (
OH
∙
) and superoxide radicals (
O 2
∙ −
) in the degradation process, directly linked to the active sites provided by the single-atom Ni. Additionally, the impressive NTC's photocatalytic performance is largely attributed to the significant role of CQDs, whose UCPL effect enhances the visible-light-driven activity of Ni-doped TiO2. These findings highlight the transformative impact of single-atom catalysts suggesting a promising direction for developing advanced materials for environmental improvement applications.
